title: Authentication Platform Semantic Contract
description: Draft semantic contract for the authentication platform slice carried from historical identity, authorization, audit, proof, and UI source evidence. Complete facets cite explicit source-import paths; state, validation, and error semantics remain draft and source-backed for authentication-slice extraction.
aiGuidance: Use this contract as a source-backed draft semantic target. Do not infer additional authentication behaviour from source files without promoting that intent into a reviewed semantic instance.

lifecycle.description: Spans the full platform sign-in surface: login initiates a PKCE/state/nonce redirect handoff, the callback validates state/code/nonce and resolves identity into a server-side session, session reads return authenticated or unauthenticated context, and logout idempotently destroys session and clears host-only plus domain-scoped cookies. It also covers IdP and policy administration lifecycles (list/create/update/rotate-secret/enable/disable/delete identity providers, OIDC discovery import, and read/validate/write of MFA, lockout, session, and claim-mapping policy). Each administrative mutation resolves the tenant credential, audits, then writes to the identity provider and may be read back.

stateModel.description: Authentication state moves unauthenticated -> authenticating -> authenticated or denied, materialised as a session record carrying session id, user id, tenant and organisation id, roles, resolved permission bundle, display name, and expiry, with no-membership and system-admin sessions holding empty tenant context by design. Administrative state includes per-tenant identity-provider definitions (alias, enabled, masked secret, scopes, issuer/JWKS validation result), OIDC discovery validation states, MFA mode (none/optional/required with TOTP or WebAuthn), lockout thresholds, and session lifespan policy. Custom-domain callback derivation depends on a domain's verified-and-active state, falling back to the platform realm for inactive or unknown hosts.

permissions.description: The login/session path establishes the actor rather than requiring a permission, converting resolved roles into an explicit permission bundle where tenant roles come from membership and system-admin comes from realm roles without tenant membership. All authentication-administration routes are tenant-scoped and require read or write auth-settings permission with the matching admin scope, resolving the tenant credential from the FQDN-derived tenant context so one tenant can never read or mutate another tenant's provider, policy, or mapping configuration. Custom-domain callback eligibility is gated by tenant domain-write lifecycle operations.

contracts.description: The operation surface covers login initiation, provider-callback handling, logout, server-side session lookup, an internal forward-auth check, and provider enumeration, plus tenant-scoped administration operations for identity-provider list/create/update/delete, OIDC discovery import, callback-URL derivation, non-interactive connection test, claim/role mapping read and full-replacement write, and MFA/lockout/session policy read and write. Mutations return a no-content success and reads return redacted projections; provider secrets and raw tokens are never exposed across the contract.

validation.description: Login sanitises the return-to target, emits PKCE challenge parameters, binds the callback to the pre-auth cookie, and rejects missing or unknown state/code and provider error callbacks while accepting only stored session cookies. Administrative validation enforces provider allowlists, required and safe-scheme OIDC URLs, reserved-alias rules, discovery issuer and JWKS validity (allowing plain HTTP only for local hosts), MFA enum values, positive lockout thresholds, bounded session lifespans, non-dangerous claim names, and realm roles restricted to the tenant-role allowlist; secrets are preserved when omitted on update and invalid bodies stop before any audit or provider mutation.

errorModel.description: All failures are fail-closed: an unauthenticated session lookup returns an unauthenticated/401 result, callback failures redirect to the login page with a generic sign-in error, and session-store lookup failure falls through to unauthenticated without leaking provider detail. Administrative failures map to typed outcomes such as validation error, no-tenant, no-credential, invalid-credential, forbidden-realm-operation, realm-unreachable, not-found, issuer-mismatch, JWKS-invalid, unreachable, and invalid-document; provider failure returns a typed degraded/readiness result, keeps mutation boundaries closed, and never fabricates successful provider state, and inactive or unknown custom hosts degrade to the platform realm rather than being trusted.

auditModel.description: Login and session creation are represented by authentication events and session-store state, and logout removes session state, while read-only session lookup and discovery validation carry no mutation audit. Every administrative mutation (provider create/update/delete/test, MFA, lockout, session, and claim-mapping changes) emits an audit-before-change event recording alias, operation, changed field names, and secret-changed booleans only; configuration and secret values are never audited, and audit failure aborts the mutation before the provider is touched.

readinessModel.description: Readiness for the live session path depends on identity-provider, session-store, and configuration availability, with an explicit deterministic fixture mode for tests; administrative readiness is credential-derived and only reports configured/ok when the tenant realm credential can read and mutate policies, otherwise classifying missing-credential, invalid-credential, forbidden-realm-operation, realm-unreachable, or discovery-invalid rather than hiding them. Degraded mode disables provider-dependent mutation paths while keeping read-only readiness and audit visible, and real brokered/interactive IdP login is carried as externally limited rather than fake-passed.

proof.description: Substrate and unit proofs cover login routes, the auth usecase, session fixtures, cookie security, token crypto, role/permission resolution, and audit-first ordering, while runtime proofs validate live identity-provider settings round-trips, IdP CRUD with secret rotation and redaction, OIDC discovery/JWKS/callback/test-connection, MFA and session policy write/read-back/restore, the claim-mapping semantic journey, and custom-domain callback derivation against local substrate. Proof is hermetic/runtime lineage; fresh USF proof pending USF-101/USF-59.
